name: Ahmet Ertegün
abstract: Ahmet Ertegun ( AH-met AIR-tə-gən; Turkish: Ahmet Zahrettin Sebuhi Ertegün, pronounced [ahˈmet æɾteˈɟyn]; July 31, 1923 – December 14, 2006) was a Turkish-American businessman, songwriter, record executive and philanthropist. Ertegun was the co-founder and president of Atlantic Records.

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: [(note)] *[Other sources suggest that this more likely was Ahmet Ertegun, rather than Nesuhi. --P.W.]

about_quotes.speaker: Tiny Grimes
about_quotes.evidence: at that time I had made some records for Atlantic. See it helped start Atlantic Records off... Ahmet Ertegun, Herb Abrams, he had a little studio called A-1 studio. And the three of us sittin' there, and that's where Atlantic Records got started.

about_quotes.speaker: George Benson
about_quotes.evidence: When I met him, I knew why. He was the biggest Benson fan, and he was a good friend of John Hammond and a lot of other people who wished me well.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Benson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Tom Malone
about_quotes.evidence: I was an arranger for a show called, a TV special called “The Fortieth Anniversary of Atlantic Records.” ... Ahmet Ertegun comes in the dressing room and he says, “No concert of Atlantic Records would be complete without Bobby Darin being represented.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom Malone interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 3, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Paul Shaffer
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Did you guys get that from Ahmet Ertegun from Atlantic Records... [INTERVIEWEE] Well I’m not sure, you know I’m not sure where they got Sir Ian.
about_quotes.doc_title: Paul Shaffer interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, August 24, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Wormworth
about_quotes.evidence: She wrote a letter to Ahmet Ertegun... my father recorded for Atlantic in 1958 with Les Jazz Modes and your late brother Nesuhi was the A&R man.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Wormworth interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 6, 2002
